summary: Brazilian Neo-Concretist artist (1920–1988). Best known for Bichos (hinged aluminum sculptures viewers manipulate), Walking/Caminhando (cutting a Mobius strip), and Nostalgia of the Body series. Co-founded Neo-Concrete movement with Helio Oiticica and Lygia Pape, inspired by Max Bill. Her essay 'Nostalgia of the Body' defines Neo-Concretism as treating art as an 'almost-body.'

comprehensive_summary:
Lygia Clark (1920–1988) was a Brazilian Neo-Concretist artist, a leading figure of Brazil's Neo-Concrete movement alongside Helio Oiticica and Lygia Pape. The movement was inspired by a 1951 retrospective on Max Bill, a Bauhaus-trained Swiss artist. Neo-Concretism, articulated in Clark's essay "Nostalgia of the Body," treated art not as a "machine" or "object" but as an "almost-body" (or "quasi-corpus").

Clark is best known for her Bichos (1960s) — a series of hinged aluminum sculptures that viewers can fold, turn, and orient in various combinations. The title means "critters" in Portuguese. She also created Walking (Caminhando), a participatory work in which viewers cut a Mobius strip lengthwise repeatedly until it is too thin to continue. Her Nostalgia of the Body series includes a piece requiring two participants to bind their hands together with a Mobius strip. In Anthropophagic Drool, participants slowly remove yarn from their mouths.

Clark's early painting Unity No. 1 depicts a square with a white border that projects slightly from the wall. Her works are shown at MASP (the Museu de Arte de São Paulo, designed by Lina Bo Bardi). She shares the first name "Lygia" with fellow Brazilian Neo-Concretist Lygia Pape, who created Divisor (a large cotton sheet with holes for people to poke their heads through). Clark appears in quizbowl mostly as bonus answers, typically clued in the context of Brazilian art, Neo-Concretism, or the Mobius strip.
